chapters.title: How the console came to be
chapters.text:
Video games are a huge part of daily life now, from phones to home consoles. They pull in large sums of money, fuel the economy, and bring fun to millions. They were not always so polished.

Back then, playing a video game meant sitting alone at a personal computer. The idea of a console, or of sharing a screen with friends, felt far off, almost a dream. For some, that dream became a steady push to make it real.

The video game industry grew slowly at first, but the spark was there from the start.

In the 1950s, the first signs of home consoles began to appear. They were just simple games that ran on several computers, far from what we know today, but they pointed the way for anyone who wanted to share a screen. The seed of the console was planted.

Those early tries to bring games into the home cleared the path for the console to be born.

Ralph Baer built the first working console. He named it the Brown Box, and it came with games ranging from checkers to several sports. It used switches and program cards so the player could pick the game. His work moved gaming into the living room and gave others a model to follow.

The console wars truly took off when the first home console was sold in 1977, but the real heavy hitters had not joined yet.

The design was so clever that others tried to copy it. In 1977, the first to make a real splash was Atari, founded by Nolan Bushnell. The original name was the Video Computer System, but buyers knew it as the Atari 2600. Atari had a strong run, but it ended up as a side note in the long arc of the console wars. The next chapters dig into the fights and the surprises that brought other brands to the top. Every war has winners and losers, and the next chapters show how fast the lead can change hands.

chapters.title: The first shakeout and Nintendo rises
chapters.text:
What looked like a golden age for gaming turned into a bloodbath. After Atari opened the door, other firms piled in to chase the best home console. The floodgates swung wide, and the shelves filled with consoles of wildly different quality.

The arrival of the Atari console made space for many new companies and their machines.

The new wave included Mattel, Coleco, and Nintendo. With all of them in play, you would expect a real fight, but most of them were in too much of a rush, churning out game after game. That rush would hurt the market as a whole.

New firms tried to grab a slice of the video game money, but they let quality slip.

The market paid the price for the flood. Games shipped with less care, less polish, and less pull. Buyers did not line up for the new releases, and those who bought them stopped playing fast. Sales sank, the gold rush ended, and many firms hit the floor.

The crash in the video game market cleared a path for Nintendo to grab the lead.

Nintendo came in with sharp marketing and a fresh take on design. They set out to reset what the American buyer expected from a console, in a pool of look alike machines. The result was the Nintendo Entertainment System, one of the sharpest 8 bit consoles of its time, and a release that locked Nintendo in as a true giant of the field. The NES had the market to itself for years, since no one else could stand up to a crash that had wiped the floor.

By 1990, Nintendo of America had sold close to thirty million consoles. One in three U.S. homes owned an NES, as Blake J. Harris notes.

chapters.title: Sega shows up swinging
chapters.text:
Nintendo had become a giant of the field, with sharper graphics and a stronger game line up than anyone else. They kept the lead for six years, raking in profit in the United States and abroad. Nintendo looked unbeatable, or so it seemed.

With no real challenger, Nintendo ran the table and pulled in record profits through the 1990s.

Then Sega came along. Sega was not brand new, but the brand had not yet built the kind of name that made players care. Nintendo, sitting on top, would need a real plan and strong products to be knocked down. Sega was ready.

Sega arrived as the new kid and gave Nintendo a real fight.

In 1993, Sega pushed a hard hitting campaign for the Sega Genesis. It was a straight attack on the public image of Nintendo and the NES. The Genesis offered sharper graphics, more game modes, fresher titles, and a faster feel. Sega was there to stay, and not in a quiet way.

Sega had to pull out all the stops to break Nintendo's grip.

That was the start of the console wars. Two giants dug in for a fight at home and abroad. Nintendo still held the lead in foreign markets, but Sega's bold ads and packaging gave American buyers a fresh feel they had been waiting for.

As Blake J. Harris puts it, video games were for everyone, and people were about to figure that out.

chapters.title: Sony steps in and rewrites the fight
chapters.text:
As the video game industry grew and Sega and Nintendo kept selling, more serious players lined up to grab the throne. At the front of the pack was the Japanese firm Sony. Sony had first tried to work with Nintendo as a partner. Seeing the brand and the profit ahead, Sony offered to bring CD technology into the consoles that still used cartridges.

Sony first wanted to ride the wave, not shape it, by helping Nintendo grow.

The deal fell apart, and the two firms went cold. From the ashes of that broken tie, a fresh idea was born. Sony had wanted to help Nintendo add CDs, but they also wanted to bring players into 3D graphics, since the rivals were still locked in 2D.

Nintendo felt that a switch to CDs would hurt its image.

After Nintendo said no, choosing to lean on what was already working, Sony saw its own shot and took it. They built a console with CDs and 3D graphics, and they called it the PlayStation. Its launch opened a new chapter for the industry and the console wars, which had lived in a flat 2D world for years. Sony was set to take over, and they did, in a big way.

Sony went back to the drawing board and turned their old offer into the PlayStation.

Nintendo still earned its share of the money, but Sega crashed hard when it tried to copy Sony's CD approach. Sega's machine sold a weak 9.5 million units. Sony topped that in the first year alone, with over 100 million units, well above Nintendo's 33 million. A new war had begun, and a new player had the bigger arsenal and was not pulling punches.

A side note for the history books: Nintendo invented the D-Pad back in 1982.

chapters.title: The price of losing ground
chapters.text:
As the war kept going, the older players fought to stay in the game. While Sega and Nintendo struggled to hold on to their buyers, Sony swept the streets and left little for anyone else.

With fresh tech on their side, Sony pulled in record profits and left only scraps for the rest of the field.

Sony released the PlayStation 2 in 2000, and it went on to become the highest selling console of all time, with a massive 150 million units moved. The win came from steady invention and a willingness to grow with the times. They moved on from the CD era of the first PlayStation to DVD in the PlayStation 2, giving players hands on access to a new kind of media that was spreading fast.

Owning a PlayStation meant owning the newest tech on the block, and that gave Sony a real edge. The DVD upgrade locked in their lead and forced the field to try to copy it. Sega kept trying to match Sony, putting out the Sega Genesis in 1988, a console that worked with CDs but did not reach Sony's level. The Sega Dreamcast in 1998 was their last try, and the last console they would make before they stepped out of the war. Their exit left room for new players to jump in.

After a long run of failed comebacks, Sega had to call it quits.

Microsoft stepped in with the first Xbox, right as Sega officially stepped out of the race on March 1, 2001. Nintendo kept chasing with the GameCube. Neither of them sold enough to worry Sony, since Sony had the new golden goose, the PlayStation 2.

chapters.title: The modern fight and old names return
chapters.text:
Years passed, and Sony was no stranger to big wins, but it was not shy about a fresh fight. The new challenger came from Microsoft and its well known Xbox line. Xbox learned from the first model and worked to match Sony on tech and on the range of games.

Microsoft learned from past mistakes and pushed harder to make better consoles.

Sony kept its pace and released the PlayStation 3. Microsoft answered with the Xbox 360, a close match that carried one extra benefit. It was cheaper. Sony leaned on its brand and tried to squeeze every last dollar from buyers. The high price on the PlayStation 3 opened the door for the Xbox 360 to grab ground.

That move locked in Microsoft as a real player in the new console wars and started a rivalry that is still alive today. In the middle of that fight, an older name was plotting a comeback, and it was only a matter of time before it found its moment.

In November 2006, Nintendo rose from the ashes with the Wii, a simple but brilliant machine. Nintendo did not want to fight on raw tech or design. Instead, it brought a fresh twist. While Microsoft and Sony were squaring off on specs, Nintendo gave players a clear, easy design that anyone could pick up.

Nintendo shot back to the top with smart marketing and fresh thinking.

That choice meant the Wii reached more than the usual buyer. Grandparents and kids picked it up as easily as core gamers. Nintendo climbed back to the top, free from the spec war that Sony and Microsoft were stuck in, and sold over 100 million units worldwide.

Today, Sony and Microsoft have found ways to compete without losing buyers over the price tag. The fight goes on. The rules of the game stay the same, but the weapons will keep changing. Who knows. A new player could step up and unseat the three giants, Sony, Microsoft, and Nintendo.

chapters.title: Looking back
chapters.text:
The console wars were born from a need to bring the joy of gaming into the living room, in a simple, ready to use form. The console was the result of years of hard work and research. But the invention led to a rough kind of competition. The first to make a stand was Atari in 1977, but that only opened the door for more players, and a flood of products hit the market.

Some of those products, if not all, were rushed and weak. Quality was an afterthought. The reckless pace helped cause the crash.

The crash left no one standing and cleared a path for a fresh challenger to step in. Nintendo made its first move in the console wars with the Nintendo Entertainment System, then a cut above the rest. The NES thrived until it owned the video game market, with over 50 million units sold at home and abroad. With that giant running the show, you would think no one would dare to fight back, but the size of the target only made it more attractive. Sega came in from Japan and made itself heard by going after the top dog, Nintendo, and that started the first real rivalry. The fight grew into a full war, and Sega crashed and burned when better armed rivals, Sony and Microsoft, joined the field. Sony and Microsoft are still the ones to beat.

As Blake J. Harris writes, video games were not just for kids. They were for anyone who wanted to feel like a kid again.

A short note to try. Sony keeps the lead by working hard to make the best gaming experience, and for this writer, they are the standout pick. You should make up your own mind. Who knows. One day we may end up on the same virtual team or fighting a cosmic giant side by side.
